title: Add UI/CLI affordance to scaffold .warren/ in a project repo
description:
Today the .warren/ convention (SPEC §11.H, R-02) is fully loader-side: warren reads .warren/triggers.yaml and .warren/defaults.json from a project clone but has no producer-side affordance. Users (including warren-on-warren dogfooding — see this repo, which until now had no .warren/ despite shipping the convention) have to:

1. Know the file names + schema
2. Hand-author triggers.yaml / defaults.json
3. git add + commit + push from the project repo

Loader contract: src/warren-config/load.ts (envelope, error codes)
Schema: src/warren-config/schema.ts (zod, strict; only kind: cron implemented)

## Proposed surfaces

Pick one (or both):

**(A) CLI: `warren init [--project <id>|--cwd]`**
Scaffolds .warren/defaults.json (defaultRole pre-filled from registered agents — prompt if multiple) and an empty .warren/triggers.yaml with the canonical comment header. Refuses to overwrite. No git side-effects — operator commits.

**(B) UI: "Initialize .warren/" button on ProjectDetail when envelope is fully null**
Posts to a new endpoint that writes files into the project clone, commits with a synthetic author, pushes to the project's default branch. Adds a write path warren doesn't currently have — needs explicit gate on push permissions and surfaces non-trivial failure modes (push reject, dirty worktree, auth). Heavier than CLI.

CLI-only is the lower-risk V1 cut. UI scaffolding could ride on R-07 (whichever issue eventually adds project-repo write paths beyond `git push` from reap).

## Acceptance
- Running the affordance against a project with no .warren/ produces a valid envelope on next GET /projects/:id/warren-config
- Schema is enforced at scaffold time (no malformed files written)
- Refuses to overwrite existing files (suggest manual edit)
- Acceptance scenario covers scaffold → load round-trip

## Related
- SPEC §11.H
- pl-5d74 (R-02 shipping plan)
- ROADMAP R-02 (shipped), R-06 (cron dispatch, just landed)

title: Add pi built-in agent + multi-provider/cost surface — warren side of pi as first-class runtime
description:
Warren side of adding pi as a third first-class runtime. Depends on burrow-side work (new piRuntime in burrow's AgentRegistry).

Scope sketch (parity baseline):
- New file: src/registry/builtins/pi.ts — AgentDefinition mirroring sapling.ts (name: 'pi', sections: { system, burrow_config }).
- Add piRuntime registration to burrow's expected agents list (burrow forwards `agents: ['pi']` already supports it — string[] contract).
- Acceptance scenario covering pi run in scripts/acceptance/scenarios/.

Beyond parity (the real work — separate phases):
1. **Customization surface.** New canopy section conventions (`pi_skills`, `pi_prompts`, eventually `pi_extensions`) that burrow's piRuntime.prepareWorkspace materializes into .pi/skills/, .pi/prompts/, .pi/extensions/ inside the sandbox workspace. Extensions as TS source strings is awkward — consider canopy artifact type instead.
2. **Multi-provider.** AgentDefinition.frontmatter.provider/model fields; POST /runs accepts overrides; UI/API gain a provider selector. Auth plumbing widens past ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (PI_*, OPENAI_API_KEY, GEMINI_API_KEY etc. via burrow envPassthrough).
3. **Cost tracking.** New columns on runs: cost_usd, tokens_input/output/cache_read/cache_write. Populated from pi's get_session_stats. UI surfaces per-run cost. This is the 'get more from raw API spend' product surface.
4. **Event-model widening.** Add compaction/retry event kinds to UI rendering (depends on burrow widening its RuntimeEvent union).

Out of scope (deferred): in-process SDK integration, extension-UI dialog escalation to warren's web UI, MCP reconciliation (pi explicitly omits MCP — worldview difference worth a brief decision).

Research feeding this seed lives in chat transcript on 2026-05-11 (Warren session): warren runtime architecture map (src/registry/builtins/sapling.ts:26-38, src/runs/spawn.ts:186, src/registry/schema.ts:50-56), pi SDK + RPC surface, and a phased build proposal.

**This seed should be converted into a detailed sd plan once internal/external research is completed** (prototype one .pi/skill canopy-section authoring flow; decide whether multi-provider is a separate seed or part of this one; spec the cost columns).

title: Land burrow piRuntime in burrow's AgentRegistry (cross-repo coordination)
description:
Cross-repo coordination seed — pins the warren↔burrow contract for landing pi as a first-class runtime. Owns the gating relationship; the implementation lives in burrow's plan pl-5198.

## Burrow tracking

- Parent: burrow-8aff — Add piRuntime to AgentRegistry (closed 2026-05-13)
- Plan: burrow pl-5198 (template: feature, 8 steps — all closed)
  - burrow-01f7 — capture pi v0.74.0 RPC stdout fixture
  - burrow-d949 — src/runtime/parsers/pi.ts
  - burrow-4a3b — src/runtime/pi.ts (piRuntime)
  - burrow-e950 — register piRuntime in BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES
  - burrow-56bb — end-to-end dispatcher test
  - burrow-c0d5 — install pi in .devcontainer/Dockerfile
  - burrow-988b — RPC-handshake compatibility test (golden fixture)
  - burrow-2fc4 — SPEC §14.1 footnote + CHANGELOG + follow-ups

## Pinned piRuntime contract (warren's expectations — all honored in burrow v0.2.8)

Warren forwards `agent.name` → burrow runtime `id` at dispatch (mx-7352f4, mx-b0a02c) and seeds the workspace via `agents: ["pi"]` in `POST /burrows` (mx-c8fc41). Burrow v0.2.8 ships piRuntime with:

- `id: "pi"` ✅
- `displayName: "Pi"` ✅
- `supportsResume: false` (V1 one-shot via `--no-session`; resume parity is a follow-up — see burrow CHANGELOG §[0.2.8])
- Spawn shape: `pi --mode rpc --no-session --no-extensions --provider anthropic --model claude-haiku-4-5` (forced argv prefix, locked by burrow-988b golden RPC-handshake test)
- RPC commands warren depends on: `prompt`, `steer`, `abort`, `get_state`, `get_session_stats` — all surfaced via the JSONL command/event protocol
- Event kinds parseEvents surfaces (warren-70af renders these in RunDetail): pi's wider RPC vocabulary collapses into burrow's stable kinds (`text`, `thinking`, `tool_use`, `tool_result`, `telemetry`, `state_change`) with the original `agent_*`, `turn_*`, `message_*`, `tool_execution_*`, `queue_update`, `compaction_*`, `auto_retry_*`, `extension_error`, `extension_ui_request`, `response` envelope preserved in `payload` — see burrow SPEC §14.1 footnote for the full collapse map
- `envPassthrough` (warren-f8c0): burrow v0.2.8 piRuntime carries the minimal `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` / `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` / `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` triple matching the forced `--provider anthropic`. Multi-provider widening lives on warren's supervisor side (warren-f8c0 forwards the broader OPENAI/GEMINI/GOOGLE/PI/GROQ/MISTRAL/DEEPSEEK key set into the container, and burrow's per-project `[env].passthrough` opens the rest per project)
- `installCheck`: `pi --version` → InstallCheckResult via shared `runVersionCheck`; populates `SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths`

## Closure gate — all conditions met

1. **burrow-8aff is closed.** [met 2026-05-13]
2. **Burrow ships a tagged release whose runtime image includes pi and registers `piRuntime` in `BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES`.** [met 2026-05-13] — `v0.2.8` tagged + GitHub release published at <https://github.com/jayminwest/burrow/releases/tag/v0.2.8>; `git show v0.2.8:src/runtime/registry.ts` shows piRuntime in `BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES`; `.devcontainer/Dockerfile` installs `@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent@0.74.0`.
3. **A live `POST /burrows {"agents": ["pi"]}` against that release succeeds (warren acceptance scenario 16).** [met 2026-05-13] — warren bumped to `@os-eco/burrow-cli ^0.2.8` (commit `7fd6050`); scenario 16 (`scripts/acceptance/scenarios/16-pi-parity-smoke.ts`) dispatches `POST /runs agent=pi` end-to-end and asserts `burrowId`/`burrowRunId` populated + ≥1 event landed. The acceptance harness registers a declarative pi stub in `burrow-with-stub.ts` for determinism (no pi binary required in CI); production warren talks to burrow 0.2.8's real piRuntime. Tightening to `kind='agent_start'` once the harness installs pi is tracked as a follow-up.
4. **The cross-repo PR link is recorded back into this seed before closure.** [met — see below]

## Cross-repo PR link

See burrow-62ce for full original context; this seed deliberately keeps the description short and points at burrow as the source of truth for the design.

title: Reposition docs from 'os-eco frontend' to standalone agent platform
description:
Warren's current public framing positions it as "the UI/control plane for
the os-eco data-plane tools" (see warren/README.md, warren/SPEC.md §1,
warren/CLAUDE.md, package.json description). This caps adoption at people
who already adopted canopy/mulch/seeds/sapling — a strict subset of the
addressable market for "an agent orchestration platform."

Reality of dependencies:
- canopy — load-bearing (agents are canopy bundles), but this is
  conceptually just "agents need config" and can be framed that way
- mulch — fully optional. Runs work with no expertise reap.
- seeds — fully optional. Cron + UI + CLI dispatch all work without seeds;
  the close-done-seeds reap step is a no-op when no seeds exist.
- sapling — fully optional. claude-code is the default harness.

So only canopy is a hard floor. The other three are *features* the user
opts into, not infrastructure they must adopt to use warren.

## Goals

1. Rewrite the user-facing positioning to lead with the product
   ("spawn custom agents at your repos, watch them work live, steer
   them, see the result") instead of the composition ("composes the
   four os-eco data-plane tools").
2. Make the fresh-install path standalone: register claude-code → add
   a GitHub URL → run a prompt → see events → see a branch. No canopy
   authoring tutorial, no mulch setup, no seeds, no sapling decision
   on first contact.
3. Surface the os-eco integrations as built-in *features* that light
   up when used: "built-in agent memory (mulch)", "built-in issue
   tracker (seeds)", "built-in prompt library (canopy)", "built-in
   steerable harness (sapling)" — each opt-in, each with its own
   onboarding path users find when they want it.
4. Reap UI/output should be silent when there's nothing to reap —
   don't show empty mulch/seeds panels for projects that don't use
   them.

## Files in scope

- warren/README.md (primary public surface — biggest rewrite)
- warren/SPEC.md §1 "Mission" and §2 "Positioning"
- warren/CLAUDE.md (orient new agents to the standalone framing too)
- warren/package.json `description`
- warren/Dockerfile comments / fly.toml comments
- warren/src/ui/ landing/login pages — any copy that name-drops the
  four tools as requirements
- Any "Getting Started" prose elsewhere

## Non-goals

- Removing the os-eco integrations from the codebase. They stay baked
  in — same code, same depth. This is a positioning/UX/docs change,
  not an architectural one.
- Renaming/restructuring CLAUDE.md sections that document concrete
  integrations (those are correct as-is — they describe what's there).
- Changing which tools are bundled in the Dockerfile. They stay
  bundled.

## Suggested narrative shape (for the README rewrite)

- One-liner: what warren *does* (spawn custom agents, watch them
  work, steer them, see results)
- Quickstart: claude-code + GitHub URL + prompt, no other tools
  mentioned
- "Power features" section: each os-eco integration as an opt-in
  feature with a short pitch and a link to its own docs

## Background

See conversation in this branch (deep-dive comparison with runtm)
for the full reasoning. Key user concern: "I'm worried about warren
adoption if it truly requires and is advertised as requiring mulch,
seeds, sapling, canopy etc."

title: Per-project provider/model defaults in .warren/defaults.json
description:
Today `.warren/defaults.json` only carries `defaultRole` / `defaultBranch` / `defaultPrompt` (see src/warren-config/schema.ts DefaultsConfigSchema). To pin a provider/model without editing the built-in agent's frontmatter (src/registry/builtins/*.ts) or picking in the UI on every dispatch, operators have to override per-run via POST /runs.

Add optional `defaultProvider` / `defaultModel` fields to DefaultsConfigSchema and have the NewRun UI + CLI run path merge them in the same precedence slot as per-run overrides (operator override > project default > agent frontmatter).

Motivating case: switching the local warren install to use pi instead of claude-code (defaults.json edit lands the agent name, but provider/model still default to whatever the pi runtime picks). Per-project pin of provider=anthropic, model=claude-opus-4-7 would close the gap without mutating the shared pi builtin.

title: spawnRun: forward agent.frontmatter as burrow run metadata so piRuntime gets provider/model
description:
warren-618b (project defaults) and burrow-b5b4 (piRuntime reads ctx.frontmatter) both landed, but the wire between them is unhooked: src/runs/spawn.ts:195-201 calls dispatchRun(...) with input.metadata, and no spawnRun call site sets it. Burrow's src/runner/dispatch.ts:130 reads frontmatter off Run.metadataJson.frontmatter — when warren sends undefined, ctx.frontmatter is undefined and buildPiArgv falls back to PI_DEFAULT_MODEL (claude-haiku-4-5).

Reproduced on this stack (warren main HEAD + burrow-cli 0.2.9 image):
- .canopy/agent.json in the workspace correctly carries frontmatter.provider=anthropic, frontmatter.model=claude-opus-4-7 (writeAgentJson seed.ts:107 already includes it)
- live pi process argv: `node /usr/local/bin/pi --mode rpc --session-dir .pi/sessions --no-extensions --provider anthropic --model claude-haiku-4-5`
- runs.rendered_agent_json on the row reflects opus correctly — the freeze is right; only the burrow handoff is wrong.

Fix sketch in spawnRun (src/runs/spawn.ts):
  const burrowRun = await dispatchRun(
    input.burrowClient, burrow.id, agent.name,
    composeDispatchPrompt(agent.sections.system, input.prompt),
    { ...(typeof input.metadata === 'object' && input.metadata !== null ? input.metadata : {}), frontmatter: agent.frontmatter },
  );

Pass the post-override agent.frontmatter (which already has operator override > project default > builtin frontmatter folded in via withProviderOverrides at line 159). Add a test in spawn.test.ts asserting the dispatchRun metadata arg contains frontmatter.provider / frontmatter.model.

Separate observation worth recording but probably a different seed: even with --model claude-haiku-4-5 the pi process sat in ep_poll for 5+ minutes producing zero RPC events. Whether that's a pi auth/handshake issue or burrow stdio attachment is out of scope for this seed.
closeReason: spawnRun now folds agent.frontmatter into burrow run metadata via composeBurrowMetadata so burrow's piRuntime can read provider/model from Run.metadataJson.frontmatter (burrow-b5b4). Verified by 3 spawn.test.ts cases asserting metadata.frontmatter on POST /burrows/:id/runs.

title: detectRuntimeTerminal doesn't recognize pi's agent_end → bridge never breaks → reap never fires
piStats agent_end check uses event.kind, but pi parser emits kind=state_change
description: Follow-up from warren-2687. src/runs/stream.ts:199 fires the piStats terminal snapshot on `event.kind === "agent_end"`, but burrow's pi parser (src/runtime/parsers/pi.ts:86-98) emits agent_end with `kind=state_change`, `stream=system`, `payload.type='agent_end'` — same envelope detectRuntimeTerminal had to be widened for. So on real pi runs the piStats branch never fires; cost/token columns silently stay null. Acceptance scenario 16 (pi-parity-smoke) doesn't currently assert on attached stats so this regression is invisible there. Fix is parallel to warren-2687: change the predicate from `event.kind === "agent_end"` to inspect the same `kind=state_change/stream=system/payload.type='agent_end'` shape that detectRuntimeTerminal now matches. Existing piStats tests in src/runs/stream.test.ts use synthetic `{kind: 'agent_end'}` shapes and would all need to be rewritten to the wire shape. Bonus: extend scenario 16 to assert cost/token columns are non-null after a pi run.

title: Acceptance fixtures leak Warren/acceptance@warren.invalid into the live project clone's .git/config
description:
**Symptom:** Commits made by agents in a warren container running against a project that has *also* had acceptance scenarios run against it carry author/committer:

```
Warren <acceptance@warren.invalid>
```

The .invalid TLD (RFC 2606) is intentionally non-routable, so these commits don't link to any GitHub account in the contribution graph.

**Reproduced** on run_62en58tednxy:
```
$ docker exec warren cat /data/projects/jayminwest/warren/.git/config
...
[user]
    email = acceptance@warren.invalid
    name = Warren
```

Pi did NOT set these values during the run (events table has zero 'git config' calls). Burrow's per-burrow identity mechanism (burrow/src/git/identity.ts) writes .gitconfig.burrow in the workspace, not the project clone's .git/config. Production warren code has no literal 'Warren' string anywhere in src/.

**Source:** scripts/acceptance/lib/fixtures.ts:228-230 writes a gitconfig template body containing 'name = Warren Acceptance' / 'email = acceptance@warren.invalid'. Several acceptance scenarios also set GIT_AUTHOR_NAME / GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL via env. When an acceptance run is performed against the same project clone path that a live container later uses, the local [user] section can stick and a subsequent live agent inherits it through git worktree (worktrees share .git/config). The clone shows 'name = Warren' (not 'Warren Acceptance'), suggesting the email half is set verbatim by a fixture while the name has been clipped by a different code path or partial overwrite — worth tracing exactly which acceptance helper writes the residual.

**Suggested fixes (one or both):**
1. The acceptance harness should never write user identity into a project clone's local .git/config — confine identity manipulation to GIT_AUTHOR_* / GIT_COMMITTER_* env vars (which the scenarios already use) and a temp $HOME with a scratch .gitconfig, so nothing persists in the clone after the scenario ends.
2. warren's project-clone setup (the boot path that ensures /data/projects/<owner>/<repo>/.git exists) should strip any [user] section it didn't itself write, or unset user.email / user.name on first-touch, so stale per-clone identities don't sneak through to agents.

**Test:** in scripts/acceptance, add a teardown assertion that no scenario leaves user.email / user.name set on the project clone. If a scenario needs an identity, it should use env-var-only and never `git config user.email` against the persistent clone path.

title: Configurable production git identity for agent commits (no acceptance-fixture default)
description:
**Today** agent commits inside a warren container use whatever identity happens to be on the project clone's .git/config. With nothing configured it's the host's git identity (via burrow IdentitySpec 'user' mode); when an acceptance scenario has polluted the clone it's the acceptance fixture leak (see sibling seed). There is no first-class way for an operator to pin a per-deployment identity like 'Warren <warren@…>'.

**Operator UX should be:** set an env var pair (or a single field in .warren/defaults.json) and every agent commit goes out with that name/email. Concrete proposal — env on the supervisor:
```
WARREN_GIT_AUTHOR_NAME=Warren
WARREN_GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL=<your-github-noreply>+warren@users.noreply.github.com
```
When set, supervisor:
- writes a scratch $HOME/.gitconfig with [user] / [committer] populated
- exports GIT_AUTHOR_NAME / GIT_AUTHOR_EMAIL / GIT_COMMITTER_NAME / GIT_COMMITTER_EMAIL into the burrow + agent processes
- the GitHub noreply email format links commits to the operator's account so the contribution graph reflects agent-driven work

Burrow already supports this concept via IdentitySpec ('bot' / 'explicit' modes in burrow/src/git/identity.ts:28-31) but warren doesn't currently surface it. The wiring is small — translate WARREN_GIT_AUTHOR_NAME/EMAIL into an explicit IdentitySpec on the POST /burrows call. Falls back to today's behavior when unset.

**Documentation:** also document the github.com noreply email pattern in .env.example so operators know the right shape to use for attribution to their account.

**Related:** sibling seed about the acceptance fixture leak — these two fixes complement each other: this seed gives the operator a way to OPT IN to a per-deployment identity; the other prevents a stale acceptance identity from being the silent fallback.
closeReason: Supervisor reads WARREN_GIT_AUTHOR_NAME/EMAIL; when both set, writes [user] to /root/.gitconfig via git config --global and exports GIT_AUTHOR_NAME/EMAIL/COMMITTER_NAME/EMAIL into process.env so burrow + warren + sandbox agents inherit them. Burrow's default 'user' mode mirrors host gitconfig into workspace .gitconfig.burrow — no burrow-side change. Documented noreply pattern in .env.example. New module src/supervisor/git-identity.ts + tests.

title: Configurable warren run-branch name (default 'burrow/<id>' confuses agents into looking in the burrow repo)
description:
**Problem:** Every warren run creates an agent commit on a branch named `burrow/<bur-id>` (e.g. `burrow/bur_wp8nrcvs9z4e`). When agents see that branch in git log / PR review, they routinely assume the branch lives in the burrow repository (because of the namespace prefix) and waste turns looking there before realizing it's a warren-managed branch on the target project repo. Confirmed multiple times across this session — agents grep the burrow tree, ask 'where is this branch defined', etc.

**Current behavior:** warren never sets the branch name. `src/runs/spawn.ts:223-230` calls `client.http.burrows.up({ projectRoot, originUrl, agents, network })` with no `branch` field, so burrow falls back to its CLI default of `burrow/<id>` (documented in burrow/src/cli/main.ts:138 and the materializeProjectWorkspace contract at burrow/src/provider/local/workspace.ts:74-79).

**Good news — burrow already supports overriding it.** `HttpBurrowUpInput` (burrow/src/lib/http-client.ts:97-118) already declares `branch?: string` on the wire. Warren just doesn't pass one. So this is a warren-only change; no burrow update required.

**Proposed shape:**
1. Add `runBranchPrefix` (or similar) to DefaultsConfigSchema in src/warren-config/schema.ts. Optional, free-text but kebab/snake-case validated (mirror RoleNameSchema). Default falls back to today's 'burrow' for backward compatibility.
2. Optionally also expose `WARREN_RUN_BRANCH_PREFIX` env on the supervisor as a deployment-wide override (overridden by project default if both set, following warren-618b's precedence pattern).
3. In spawnRun (src/runs/spawn.ts:223-230), read the resolved prefix, build the branch as `${prefix}/${burrowRunId}`, and pass it via the existing `branch` field on `client.http.burrows.up`.
4. Document the new field in .env.example and SPEC §11.A (reap section names the branch convention).

Reasonable defaults to suggest in the docs: `warren`, `agent`, `bot/<agent-name>`, or the operator's GitHub username. Anything but 'burrow'.

**Test:** in src/runs/spawn.test.ts, assert that the burrows.up input carries `branch === '${configuredPrefix}/${burrowRunId}'`. Default (no config) keeps the legacy 'burrow/' prefix to avoid breaking existing deployments.

**Related:** warren-618b (the precedence model — operator override > project default > built-in default — should govern this too). warren-fe67 (git author identity) is the sibling concern: both are about how warren-driven commits are attributed and discovered.
closeReason: Warren now composes burrow branch as ${prefix}/${run.id}. Prefix resolves project (.warren/defaults.json.runBranchPrefix) > WARREN_RUN_BRANCH_PREFIX env > built-in 'burrow'. Schema field added with RoleNameSchema-style validation; env loader is lenient (invalid silently falls back). Run id suffix replaces burrow id so branches back-reference warren rows on git log / PR review. UI surfaces runBranchPrefix read-only on ProjectDetail.

title: Adopt R-07 HttpClient for burrow workspace seed + reap; remove direct-disk writes
description:
Warren today seeds burrow workspaces by writing directly to burrow.workspacePath on disk (src/runs/seed.ts) and reaps by reading directly off disk (src/runs/reap.ts). This works only because warren and burrow are co-tenanted in one container sharing /data. Every other warren↔burrow path is HTTP with a typed contract; these two reach past the seam onto disk.

Burrow's R-07 (plan pl-2467, closed 2026-05-09 in burrow) shipped the workspace-mutation HTTP surface:

  - POST /burrows with optional seed: { files: [{ path, contents, encoding?, mode? }, ...] } — atomic with provisioning, failed seed rolls the burrow back
  - POST /burrows/:id/files — post-provision writes
  - GET /burrows/:id/files?path=…&encoding=… — read-side for reap

Available on warren's existing dep @os-eco/burrow-cli ^0.2.12 via HttpClient.burrows.create({ seed }) and HttpClient.files.write / files.read.

What needs to change:

1. Seed (src/runs/seed.ts → src/runs/spawn.ts). Refactor seedBurrowWorkspace from a side-effecting writer into a pure builder that returns HttpWorkspaceFile[]. Thread the file list into provisionBurrow → burrowClient.http.burrows.up({ projectRoot, originUrl, agents, network, seed: { files } }). Single round-trip, atomic with up.

2. Reap mulch_merge (src/runs/reap.ts). Currently fs.readdir(.mulch/expertise/) + fs.readFile per domain. Burrow's R-07 has no list endpoint yet, so this step is partially blocked — file a small burrow follow-up (GET /burrows/:id/files?prefix=… or similar listing shape) and gate the reap migration on it.

3. Reap seeds_close (src/runs/reap.ts). One known file (.seeds/issues.jsonl) — can migrate to files.read immediately.

4. Reap branch_push (src/runs/reap.ts). git push from the workspace directory. Fundamentally workspace-local and only works because warren shares disk; remote burrow would need a separate primitive. Out of scope for this seed — note it as a follow-up in the warren ROADMAP.

5. Delete the disk-writing helpers (writeCanopyAgent, writeExpertiseSeed, writeWorkflowTemplate, writePiArtifacts) and the SeedFs injection seam (tests switch to asserting on the produced file list, not on a fake fs).

6. Update warren SPEC §11.A wording: replace "invokes ml record inside the burrow workspace via burrow exec (or equivalent)" with the HTTP contract reference.

7. CHANGELOG entry + minor-version bump (warren 0.1.7 → 0.2.0).

Acceptance:
- Spawn integration test exercises burrows.up with a populated seed payload end-to-end against a real burrow (or HttpClient mock); no warren-side disk writes occur during seed.
- src/runs/seed.ts contains no fs/promises imports; the SeedFs interface is removed.
- Reap seeds_close uses files.read; mulch_merge gated on burrow-side list endpoint follow-up (filed as a separate seed).
- Existing seed.test.ts + spawn.test.ts pass with the new builder shape.
- CHANGELOG.md notes the R-07 adoption and links burrow pl-2467.
closeReason: Plan pl-a31c complete: seed.ts is a pure builder (warren-e238), spawn.ts threads seed.files into burrows.up for atomic provision+seed (warren-eaee), reap seeds_close migrated to HttpClient.files.read (warren-ae92), burrow follow-up filed as burrow-18ca via warren-7f7c, SPEC/CHANGELOG/version 0.2.0 landed (warren-b894). Warren↔burrow now HTTP-only on every spawn-path file write and on reap seeds_close; mulch_merge stays disk-bound pending burrow-18ca, branch_push out of scope (would need a separate remote-burrow primitive).

title: Scenario 16: assert non-null cost/token columns after a pi run (warren-36c0 bonus)
description:
Bonus from warren-36c0: extend scripts/acceptance/scenarios/16-pi-parity-smoke.ts to assert runs.cost_usd / tokens_input / tokens_output are non-null after the pi run.

## Update 2026-05-14 (warren-17a4 in-flight)

The original framing assumed pi cost would land via an out-of-band RPC (PiStatsClient calling pi's `get_session_stats` through some burrow pass-through that doesn't exist). That was the wrong model. Pi v0.74 already emits per-turn usage inline in `turn_end` envelopes — see burrow `src/runtime/parsers/__golden__/pi-v0.74.0-anthropic-*.jsonl`:

```
{"type":"turn_end","message":{...,"usage":{"input":446,"output":44,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"cost":{"total":0.000666}}}}
```

The bridge already consumes these events. Warren-side wiring is now an in-stream accumulation in `bridgeRunStream` (src/runs/stream.ts) — no burrow surface widening needed.

**Landed in src/runs/stream.ts:**
- `accumulatePiUsage` reads `payload.type=turn_end / message.usage.{input,output,cacheRead,cacheWrite,cost.total}` and adds to a per-run accumulator.
- At `agent_end` (or terminalDetected), `persistInStreamPiUsage` calls `RunsRepo.attachStats` with the accumulated totals.
- PiStatsClient remains as an explicit override for sources warren can't observe in-stream; when both paths produce data, the explicit client wins (preserves existing piStats test contract).
- 6 new tests cover the in-stream paths.

**Remaining blocker for scenario 16 cost assertion:** the acceptance harness registers pi as a declarative agent in `scripts/acceptance/lib/burrow-with-stub.ts` with `outputFormat: "raw-text"`. Burrow's declarative agent only supports `raw-text` / `stream-json` / `jsonl-claude` (burrow `src/runtime/declarative.ts:130-134`) — there is no `jsonl-pi` outputFormat, so even if the stub script emitted pi-shaped `turn_end` JSONL, burrow would wrap each line as `{kind:"text", stream:"stdout"}` and the bridge's `turn_end` predicate wouldn't match.

Unblocking paths (pick one):
1. Add `jsonl-pi` as a declarative outputFormat in burrow (cross-repo; mirrors `jsonl-claude`).
2. Install the real `pi` binary in the acceptance environment and switch the harness to dispatch through burrow's piRuntime (real RPC + parser) — see warren-0e06 follow-up note.
3. Bypass declarative: have the harness register a built-in-style runtime wrapper that uses `parsePiEvents` directly.

Once one of those lands, scenario 16's assertion becomes a one-liner: after `waitForFirstEvent` (or a dedicated `waitForTerminal` helper), `SELECT cost_usd, tokens_input, tokens_output FROM runs WHERE id=?` and assert non-null + positive.

The predicate fix from warren-36c0 (src/runs/stream.ts now uses isPiAgentEnd) was the unblock-enabler; the in-stream extraction (warren-17a4) is the data-source resolution. Scenario 16 assertion remains the open work.

title: [warren] Per-resource reads (cancel/steer/reap/bridges) route via pool.clientFor instead of singleton
description:
Follow-up from warren-14ad / pl-9ba1 step 5. Step 5 landed the fan-out path (GET /burrows) and the targeted GET /burrows/:id via pool.clientFor. Cancel / steer / reap / bridges still consume the legacy `deps.burrowClient` singleton — functionally equivalent today with one local worker, but a no-op write surface for multi-worker placement.

These call sites all have a `run.burrowId` (or can resolve one), so each should:
1. Take a `burrowClientPool: BurrowClientPool` instead of a `burrowClient: BurrowClient`.
2. Resolve the owning worker via `pool.clientFor({ burrowId })`.
3. Propagate `StickyWorkerUnreachableError` as-is (already mapped to 503 in src/server/errors.ts after warren-14ad).

Call sites:
- src/runs/cancel.ts (cancelRun)
- src/runs/steer.ts (steerRun)
- src/runs/reap.ts (reapRun + mirrorClosedSeeds inner http.files.read)
- src/runs/stream.ts (bridgeRunStream)
- src/server/bridges.ts (createBridgeRegistry / bootBridges)
- src/cli/commands/run.ts (CLI run get)

Once all six migrate, `deps.burrowClient` and `pool.singleton()` can both go away (per the BurrowClientPool module comment).

Blocks warren-272c implicitly: a multi-worker [workers] config without per-resource clientFor would silently mis-route control-plane operations.

title: [warren] migrate residual pool.singleton() consumers (bridges/steer/cancel/boot probe) to clientFor so multi-worker [workers] boots
description: Today's bootServer derives the legacy burrowClient variable via pool.singleton() (src/server/main.ts:~110) for bridges, the boot-time burrow probe, and the steer/cancel handlers. singleton() throws on size != 1, so warren-272c's [workers] support only works for single-worker configs. To unblock pl-9ba1's two-worker integration test (warren-a801), migrate each remaining consumer to pool.clientFor({ burrowId }) (sticky-by-burrow) or pool.entries() (probe across all workers). Then delete BurrowClientPool.singleton() and the burrowClient/ServerDeps.burrowClient back-compat plumbing.

title: bootBridges crash-loops on burrow placement orphans (legacy runs from pre-pl-9ba1)
description:
## Symptom

Warren container enters a docker restart loop on boot. Every restart prints the same stack:

\`\`\`
NoEligibleWorkerError: burrow has no placement record: bur_<id>
 recoveryHint: "warren has no \`burrows\` row for this id; it may have been provisioned before multi-worker placement landed (pl-9ba1)"
  at placeForBurrow (src/runs/placement.ts:96)
  at clientFor (src/burrow-client/pool.ts:251)
  at bridgeRunStream (src/runs/stream.ts)
  at runWithReconnect (src/server/bridges.ts:183)
  at start (src/server/bridges.ts:110)
  at bootBridges (src/server/bridges.ts:316)
  at bootServer (src/server/main.ts)
\`\`\`

Supervisor sees warren exit 1 → tears down burrow → exits 1 → docker \`restart: unless-stopped\` brings it back → loop.

Discovered during the v0.3.0 dogfooding run for warren-17a4. Three pi runs (run_62en58tednxy, run_rtq0fyy4vzxj, run_xzd93aejgg4z) had been dispatched before multi-worker placement landed; their burrow_ids had no rows in the \`burrows\` table. Unblocked operationally by marking those 3 rows \`state=failed, failure_reason=crashed\` directly in /data/warren.db; warren now boots cleanly.

## Root cause (two layers)

**Layer 1 — bootBridges does not pre-flight placement.**
\`src/server/bridges.ts:289-326\` filters out runs with \`burrowRunId === null\` or \`burrowId === null\` and surfaces them via \`skipped: [{ runId, reason }]\`. It does not check that \`repos.burrows.get(run.burrowId)\` returns a row, so legacy runs whose burrow_id predates pl-9ba1's \`burrows\` placement table slip through into \`registry.start(...)\`.

**Layer 2 — registry.start fires-and-forgets without a .catch.**
\`src/server/bridges.ts:107-127\` does:
\`\`\`ts
const done = runWithReconnect({...});
const entry: BridgeEntry = { burrowRunId, abort, done };
live.set(runId, entry);
void done.finally(() => { if (live.get(runId) === entry) live.delete(runId); });
\`\`\`
The \`.finally\` runs on settle but does not catch rejection. When \`bridgeRunStream\` throws on its first \`pool.clientFor()\` call, the rejection on \`done\` is unhandled and Bun terminates the process. This is the trigger for the crash loop — even unrelated bridge-startup throws (transient pool errors, etc.) would crash the supervisor today.

## Fix shape

Both layers should be fixed; either alone is incomplete.

1. **bootBridges pre-flight (src/server/bridges.ts:289-326)** — before \`registry.start(...)\`, check \`input.repos.burrows.get(run.burrowId) !== null\` and skip with \`{ runId, reason: \"no_placement\" }\`. Mirrors the existing no_burrow_id / no_burrow_run_id skip patterns. Gives operators a clean log signal instead of an opaque crash.

2. **registry.start defensive .catch (src/server/bridges.ts:107-127)** — wrap the fired-and-forgotten \`done\` in \`.catch((err) => logger.error(...))\` before the \`.finally\`. Defends against ALL bridge-startup throws, not just the placement one. Also probably want to surface the throw as a synthetic event on the run (\`bridge_fatal\`) so the UI shows why the bridge stopped.

3. **Tests** — extend src/server/bridges.test.ts:
   - bootBridges produces \`skipped: [{ runId, reason: \"no_placement\" }]\` when the burrow_id has no \`burrows\` row.
   - registry.start does not propagate a rejection from \`bridge\` to the supervisor (mock a throwing \`bridge\`, assert no unhandled rejection).

## Notes

- Discovered while dispatching warren-17a4 (pi cost-extraction acceptance assertion). The user-visible symptom blocked dogfooding entirely.
- The mitigation applied (mark legacy runs failed) is a one-shot — same trigger will repeat any time a burrow_id is present in \`runs\` without a corresponding \`burrows\` row.
- Consider a doctor check (\`burrows-placement-consistency\`) to surface this gap proactively before it crashes a boot.

title: UI: events pane is unreadable — one-line-per-event with expand
description:
## Symptom

The Events card on `/runs/:id` (RunDetail.tsx, `EventTail` + `EventLine`) renders each event as the full stringified payload inline in a monospace block. For a typical pi run (300+ events with deeply nested `message_update` payloads, tool-call JSON, golden-fixture text) the pane becomes a wall of escaped JSON that no human can scan. Screenshot: events 321 and 322 from run_f1ecqmp5dyxj — a single `state_change system` event takes ~40 lines of wrapped text.

## Desired shape

One line per event by default, with click/keyboard expand for the full payload:

```
[321] 05:01:42  state_change  system   turn_end · message (toolCall: bash) · usage 154/23.8k · $0.026
[322] 05:01:43  state_change  system   turn_start
```

Each line should carry:
- seq (`[321]`)
- relative or wall-clock timestamp
- `kind` and `stream` (already exposed on RunEvent)
- a one-line summary derived from the payload — for `turn_end`, the agent's last tool name + usage totals; for `text` chunks, the first ~80 chars; for tool_execution_*, the tool name + exit
- Click row to expand a `<details>`/dialog with the full pretty-printed JSON

Filter chips (kind, stream) and a "show only assistant text" toggle would help further but aren't blockers.

## Fix locations

- `src/ui/src/pages/RunDetail.tsx:263-336` — `EventTail` (pane shell, scroll, autoscroll).
- `src/ui/src/pages/RunDetail.tsx` near line 330 — `EventLine` (per-event renderer, currently the offender).
- `src/ui/src/api/types.ts` — `RunEvent` shape if a summary needs richer typing.

## Acceptance

- Default row height is one line per event for every event kind warren emits today.
- Each line summary covers `turn_end`, `turn_start`, `message_start`, `message_update`, `text`, `toolcall_*`, `tool_execution_*`, `state_change`, `agent_end`, `reap.*` without resorting to raw JSON.
- Expanded view shows the original payload pretty-printed (existing JSON.stringify shape is fine, just behind a toggle).
- Vertical density >= 20 events visible at typical window heights without scroll.

title: UI: /agents expanded row scrolls horizontally on long prompts; render JSON neatly
description:
## Symptom On `/agents`, expanding an agent row to show its rendered definition causes horizontal page scroll when the agent's `sections.system` (or any long string field in the rendered JSON) is wider than the viewport. The pane uses a `
` with `JSON.stringify(agent.renderedJson, null, 2)` — long unbroken lines never wrap, the `
` widens, the table cell widens, the page horizontally scrolls.

## Fix locations

`src/ui/src/pages/Agents.tsx:163-167` — the offending render:

```tsx

  {JSON.stringify(agent.renderedJson, null, 2)}


```

## Desired shape

Two changes, smallest-first:

1. **Wrap, don't scroll horizontally.** Add `whitespace-pre-wrap break-words` (Tailwind) so long lines wrap inside the cell instead of widening the table. Constrain the `
`'s max width to the row.
2. **Render the agent neatly, not as raw JSON.** Show the agent definition as a structured panel a human can read:
   - top: `name`, `version`, `provider`, `model`, `tags`, `source` as a labelled grid
   - then each `sections.*` key as a collapsible block with a header (e.g. "System prompt", "burrow_config") and a wrapped, monospaced body
   - `resolvedFrom` as a compact breadcrumb
   - A small "View raw JSON" toggle preserves the current debug shape for parity

Step 1 alone unblocks the horizontal scroll bug; step 2 is the actual UX win.

## Acceptance

- Expanding an agent row never produces page-level horizontal scroll, regardless of prompt length.
- Long system prompts wrap at the cell width.
- Default view shows the agent definition as labelled sections, not raw stringified JSON.
- Raw-JSON view is still reachable for debug.

title: UI: surface run cost by default on /runs list and /runs/:id (now that pi populates inline)
description:
## Symptom

Run cost is technically displayed in two places but easy to miss / opt-in only:

- **`/runs` list (Runs.tsx:67-86, 159-176):** cost column is hidden behind a `Show cost` checkbox (`showCost` localStorage flag, default off). Comment at the top of the file (warren-a7dc) explains the rationale: "most agents don't carry costUsd today". That premise is changing — warren-17a4 lands in-stream pi cost extraction, so pi runs (the most common runtime today) will reliably hydrate `costUsd` going forward.
- **`/runs/:id` (RunDetail.tsx:135-141, helper at 360-388):** cost badge is present but small; tokens are formatted into a tooltip helper (`formatTokensTooltip`) and may not be visible without hover.

## Desired shape

1. **Promote cost to a default-on column** on the `/runs` list. Keep the toggle as a hide option for users who don't care, but flip the default from `off` to `on`. Format as today (`formatCostUsd`). Show `—` when null (already done). Consider sorting / total-cost-this-page.
2. **Make cost prominent on `/runs/:id`.** Lift the cost+tokens panel out of a single small badge into its own card alongside the existing summary card — same prominence as state/duration. Show tokens (in/out/cache-r/cache-w) inline, not only in a hover tooltip.
3. **Optional: aggregate totals.** Footer or header on `/runs` showing total spend over the visible filter window (today, this week). Cheap to compute client-side from the loaded page.

## Fix locations

- `src/ui/src/pages/Runs.tsx:19-27` — comment + localStorage key for `showCost`. Flip default; consider deprecating the toggle.
- `src/ui/src/pages/Runs.tsx:78-86` — `Show cost` checkbox UI.
- `src/ui/src/pages/Runs.tsx:155-176` — conditional `<TableCell>` render.
- `src/ui/src/pages/RunDetail.tsx:135-141` — current cost badge.
- `src/ui/src/pages/RunDetail.tsx:360-388` — `formatCostUsd` / `formatTokensTooltip` helpers (reuse).

## Acceptance

- Default `/runs` view shows a cost column without any user action.
- `/runs/:id` shows cost AND token breakdown in a primary card, not buried in a tooltip.
- Both null-cost states (`costUsd === null`) render as `—` not as the empty string.
- Existing pi-cost test fixtures still pass.

title: Reap pr_open: improve auto-PR title and body — commits, files, seed link, optional LLM summary
description:
## Symptom

Auto-PRs opened by reap's `pr_open` sub-step are functional but uninformative for human reviewers. Example: warren PR #7 (run `run_f1ecqmp5dyxj`, agent `pi`, prompt `"work on sd warren-17a4. use ml. commit and push to your branch when done, and open a PR into main"`):

- **Title:** `"work on sd warren-17a4. use ml. commit and push to your branch when don…"` — the full prompt truncated mid-word at 72 chars.
- **Body:** `Opened by warren run \`run_f1ecqmp5dyxj\` (pi).\n\n## Prompt\n\n\`\`\`\n<full prompt>\n\`\`\`` — no link back to warren UI (because `WARREN_BASE_URL` is unset; current code already supports it when set), no list of commits, no files changed, no reference to the seed id that appears in the prompt, no summary of what the agent actually did.

## Fix locations

`src/runs/pr.ts:185-230` — `buildPrContent(input: BuildPrContentInput): PrContent` is the single source of title + body. Test file `src/runs/pr.test.ts` exists; new shape needs corresponding fixtures.

The reap call site is `src/runs/reap.ts:413` (`openPr: input.openPr ?? openPullRequest`). The reap step has access to:
- `run.id`, `run.agentName`, `run.prompt`, `run.startedAt`, `run.endedAt`, `run.costUsd`, `run.tokensInput/Output`
- The pushed branch + base branch + repo
- The reap event stream (already accumulated by this point)

## Desired shape (Phase 1 — no new agent calls)

**Title** — priority order:
1. If the prompt mentions a seed id (regex: `\b([a-z-]+-[a-f0-9]{4,})\b` matches `warren-17a4`), shell out: `sd show <id> --format json` and use `issue.title`. Fall back through the chain on miss.
2. Else if the agent produced at least one commit on the branch, use the **first commit's subject line** (already a human-authored title for the change).
3. Else fall back to truncated prompt (current behavior).

**Body sections** — replace current minimal body with:

\`\`\`markdown
## Summary

<First commit subject, or "Agent <name> ran for <duration>; no commits."  >

## Run

- **Warren UI:** <link if WARREN_BASE_URL set, else just \`run_xxx\`>
- **Agent:** pi
- **Duration:** 4m 17s
- **Cost:** $0.13 (24.6k in / 5.2k out / 21.9k cache-r)

## Seeds

- warren-17a4 — Scenario 16: assert non-null cost/token columns after a pi run

## Commits (3)

- abc1234 fix(stream): accumulate pi turn_end usage across the run
- def5678 test(stream): cover terminalDetected flush path
- 0123456 docs(spec): note in-stream cost extraction

## Files changed (5)

src/runs/stream.ts                 | 42 +++++++++++
src/runs/stream.test.ts            | 88 ++++++++++++++++++++
scripts/acceptance/scenarios/...   | 12 ++-
...                                | ...
                                     5 files changed, 152 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

## Prompt

<collapsed <details><summary> with the original prompt — preserves the audit trail without dominating the body>

---

🤖 Opened by [warren](https://github.com/jayminwest/warren) run \`run_f1ecqmp5dyxj\`
\`\`\`

The commit list + files-changed block can come from a single \`git log --oneline\` + \`git diff --stat\` against the base branch inside the workspace before push. Reap already runs in the workspace at that point; this is cheap.

## Desired shape (Phase 2 — optional, behind WARREN_PR_SUMMARY_AGENT)

Replace the **Summary** section with an LLM-generated paragraph that reads the diff + commits and explains what changed and why. Gated by an env var so deployments that don't want extra API spend keep Phase 1 behavior. Likely a small Haiku-tier call, capped at ~200 tokens. Skip when there are zero commits or when the diff exceeds N MB. Failures fall back to the Phase 1 first-commit-subject summary.

## Acceptance

- Title is never the raw truncated prompt for runs that produced commits.
- Body includes commit list and files-changed stats whenever the branch has commits ahead of base.
- Body includes a structured Run section with cost + duration + warren UI link (when WARREN_BASE_URL is set).
- Body cross-links any seed id mentioned in the prompt to its title (best-effort; missing seed CLI is non-fatal).
- Original prompt is preserved in a collapsed <details> block.
- pr.test.ts golden tests cover at least: empty-prompt, seed-id-in-prompt, commits-present, zero-commits, and the existing TITLE_MAX_LENGTH overflow case.
- Phase 2 LLM summary is opt-in (env-gated) and has a deterministic fallback when disabled or on error.

## Related

- warren-f6af landed the original `pr_open` sub-step in reap.
- Existing `WARREN_BASE_URL` plumbing (src/runs/pr.ts:217-221) already supports the link — this seed extends, doesn't replace.

title: Re-prompt / re-trigger a terminated run — continue the same conversation against the same workspace
description:
## Want

After a run terminates (succeeded / failed / cancelled), provide a way to send a follow-up prompt and re-run *as a continuation* of that exact agent session. Today every run is one-shot: the burrow is reaped, the workspace branch is pushed, the agent process is gone. Iteration costs the operator full context re-seeding plus a fresh prompt.

Use cases:
- Reviewer says "looks close but tests are still failing — re-run with the fix" → continuation run reuses the branch, picks up where the agent left off, no re-clone.
- Agent stops short ("I'm not sure if I should change X — please confirm") → operator clarifies and resumes.
- A failed run is rerun with an updated prompt after the operator inspects events.

## Design space (pick one path)

**Option A — Re-spawn on the same branch (simplest).** New warren run, new burrow, but workspace seeded from the prior run's branch (already pushed by reap) instead of `main`. Implementation hook: `src/runs/spawn.ts` workspace prep already supports a base ref; need a UI/API surface that says "base = branch of run X". The agent restarts cold (no conversation history) but operates on the same files. Lowest-cost; aligns with how pi and claude-code already work today (stateless tools, files are the state). This is probably the right V1.

**Option B — Burrow / runtime "resume" (richest).** Keep the burrow alive past reap. Provide \`POST /runs/:id/resume\` that re-enters the runtime with a new prompt as a steering message after \`agent_end\`. Requires:
- burrow: an explicit "park, don't tear down on agent_end" mode (today reap drops the burrow).
- pi / claude-code: support for continuing a session after agent_end (pi has \`--continue\` semantics via \`PI_SESSION_DIR\`; claude-code has its own session resume). Per-runtime.
- warren: cost / token accumulation across resume rounds (cost_usd / tokens_* already nullable+additive — extend persistInStreamPiUsage to add rather than replace).
- a TTL on parked burrows so they don't pile up (e.g. drop after 24h or N MB workspace).

**Option C — Cherry-pick the conversation (compromise).** New run, but seeded with the prior run's terminal event tail as system / context prefix. Agent has the "transcript" but not literal session continuation. Cheaper than B, richer than A. Lossy for tool-state-sensitive runtimes.

## Recommendation

Ship **A** first (1-2 days). It covers ~70% of the use case (file-level iteration on a branch). Ship **B** later, gated per-runtime, where it pays off (long context / expensive priming).

## API + UI surface (Option A)

\`POST /runs\` already exists. Extend with an optional \`continueFromRunId\` (or rename to \`baseRunId\`) field. When set:
- look up prior run's pushed branch
- spawn the new run with that branch as the workspace base (skip the \`git checkout main\` step in workspace prep)
- pre-populate the new run's prompt textarea with the prior run's prompt (UI affordance only)
- record the parent link in the new run row (new column \`parent_run_id text references runs(id)\` or in \`extensions\`)

UI:
- On \`/runs/:id\` for terminal runs, add a "Re-run with follow-up" button that prefills \`/runs/new\` with \`continueFromRunId\` + the prior prompt.
- On \`/runs\`, show a small chain indicator (\`↪ from run_xxx\`) when \`parent_run_id\` is set.

## Acceptance

- Operator can click "Re-run with follow-up" on a terminated run, edit the prompt, dispatch — the new run's workspace starts from the prior branch, not \`main\`.
- The new run row links back to the parent (UI + API + DB).
- Cost / tokens on the new run are independent (not accumulated with parent) — chain totals derivable from the parent link.
- Works for every built-in agent (pi, claude-code, sapling).
- Tests in src/runs/spawn.test.ts cover base-branch override path.

## Related

- src/runs/spawn.ts owns workspace prep — branch base lives here.
- src/runs/seed.ts owns agent.json + mulch/seeds workspace seeding — re-seed is reused as-is for Option A.
- src/db/schema.ts → add parent_run_id column (or extensions.warren_parent_run_id under the existing extensions namespacing convention).
- Option B references: warren-17a4 / pl-cb3e (burrow multi-worker substrate already drained-state aware).

title: UI: read-only /config page sourced from .warren/config.yaml
description:
## Want

A read-only `/config` page in the warren UI where an operator can inspect server configuration without sshing into the container. **V1 scope is display only** — sourced from `.warren/config.yaml`, no DB-backed overrides, no editing affordances. Editing and a runtime-mutable settings store are deferred to a follow-up.

## Source of truth

A single `.warren/config.yaml` parsed at process start. The `/config` page renders what was loaded — same merged view warren itself sees, no second source-of-truth to keep in sync.

Secrets (`WARREN_API_TOKEN`, `WARREN_BURROW_TOKEN`, `WARREN_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, `WARREN_GITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY`, `GITHUB_TOKEN`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, etc.) **never leave the server.** UI shows them as `set` / `unset` badges only, never the value.

## /config page shape

One read-only view, grouped by section (Identity, Cluster, Runtime — whatever falls out of the config schema). Each row shows:
- key
- active value (or `set`/`unset` for secrets)
- source (`.warren/config.yaml` vs compiled default)

No edit affordances, no reset buttons, no save buttons. A small note at the top: "Config is read-only. Edit `.warren/config.yaml` and restart to change."

## API surface

- `GET /admin/config` — returns the parsed config with secrets redacted (`set`/`unset` only) and source attribution per key.
- Authed via the existing bearer token.

That's it for V1 — no PUT, no DELETE, no schema endpoint.

## Explicitly out of scope (V1)

- DB-backed `server_settings` table and runtime-mutable knobs
- UI editing of any config value
- TOML/YAML editor in the browser
- Reset-to-default buttons
- Audit log for config changes
- Project-scoped overrides

These belong in a follow-up seed once the read-only surface is shipped and the schema has settled.

## Acceptance

- `/config` route exists and renders the parsed `.warren/config.yaml` plus secret presence badges.
- Secrets are never returned by `GET /admin/config` — only `set`/`unset`.
- `GET /admin/config` requires the bearer token (401 without).
- No write endpoints exist; no DB schema changes; no editing UI components.

## Related

- src/server-config/ — existing config loader. V1 may need a small redaction pass for the new admin endpoint.
- Follow-up seed (to file when this lands): DB-backed runtime overrides + editing UI.

title: Real multi-burrow acceptance scenario (scripts/acceptance/scenarios/18-multi-worker.ts)
description:
Follow-up to pl-9ba1 / pl-cb3e (R-12). The in-process stub-based integration test (`src/server/integration.multi-worker.test.ts`) and the new cross-process pool integration test (`src/burrow-client/integration.cross-process.test.ts`) cover the wire envelope + the BurrowClientPool boundary against real `burrow serve` subprocesses, but neither drives the *full* operator path:

  - real `warren` HTTP server (started via `bootServer`)
  - real `warren.toml` with `[workers]` block parsed by the config loader
  - real `BurrowClientPool.fromConfig` wired by warren boot
  - real `POST /workers/:name/drain` on warren routing to `/admin/drain` on burrow
  - real `GET /burrows` fan-out via warren handler against multiple real burrows
  - real run-spawn against a real canopy fixture + project + stub-shell agent on each worker

That last bit is what makes scenario 04 / 09 / 10 work today — they boot one real burrow through `scripts/acceptance/lib/inproc.ts`'s `bootInProc` and exercise the full §4.3 composition flow. The R-12 analog needs:

## Scope

Add `scripts/acceptance/scenarios/18-multi-worker.ts` that:

1. Boots **two** real `burrow-with-stub` subprocesses (alpha + beta) on separate unix sockets in the same tmp root. Both spawned WITHOUT `--no-auth` and WITH `BURROW_API_TOKEN=<shared>`.

2. Generates a `warren.toml` with a `[workers]` block listing both unix-socket URLs, plus the shared `WARREN_BURROW_TOKEN` env var. Drops `WARREN_BURROW_SOCKET` so warren picks the `fromConfig` path.

3. Boots warren via `bootInProc` extended to support multi-burrow + toml config. The harness lib needs a new helper (e.g. `bootInProcMultiBurrow`) or `bootInProc` gains an `extraBurrows` knob.

4. Drives the three R-12 acceptance scenarios end-to-end through warren's HTTP API:
   - **Project-affinity**: dispatch run for project P → recorded on worker X. Dispatch a second run for P → lands on same X. Dispatch for project Q → lands on the other worker (least-loaded).
   - **Failover-on-drain**: `POST /workers/X/drain` on warren → next dispatch for P lands on Y. The in-flight burrow already pinned to X stays served via sticky-by-burrow.
   - **Cross-worker fan-out**: `GET /burrows` unions per-worker burrows sorted by `createdAt`. Kill one burrow process mid-list → response carries a `workerErrors` envelope plus a `worker_unreachable` log line.

5. (Stretch) **Sticky-by-burrow with killed worker**: SIGKILL alpha, attempt `GET /burrows/<alpha-burrow-id>` via warren → 502 `worker_unreachable` (not silent re-placement).

## What scaffolding to reuse

- `scripts/acceptance/lib/inproc.ts` — the in-process boot pattern (warren + burrow as sibling subprocesses). Extend to multi-burrow.
- `scripts/acceptance/lib/burrow-with-stub.ts` — the existing burrow wrapper that registers stub-shell. Already accepts `--socket` and `--no-auth`; the wrapper itself doesn't need changes (just drop `--no-auth` and pass `BURROW_API_TOKEN` env).
- `scripts/acceptance/lib/fixtures.ts` — `buildFixtures` (canopy repo + sample project) should still work; the scenario just needs to dispatch through warren's `POST /runs` against the same fixtures, twice per project.

## Why not in `bun test`

A `bun test`-runnable test of this shape would require booting warren, canopy, and two burrows inside one test process — that's exactly what the acceptance harness already orchestrates, and duplicating it under `bun test` would mean re-implementing `bootInProc` / `buildFixtures` against bun test lifecycle. The acceptance harness is the right home; `bun test` already covers the BurrowClientPool boundary via `integration.cross-process.test.ts` and the warren HTTP envelope via `integration.multi-worker.test.ts` (stubbed).

## Cross-host extension (future, not in this seed)

Compose-mode (`--mode container`) variant where the two burrows run as separate docker-compose services on a private network. That exercises TLS-at-reverse-proxy, network partition, and the actual cross-host topology R-12 is positioned for. File as a follow-up after this scenario lands.

## Acceptance

- Scenario passes against the in-proc harness: `bun run scripts/acceptance/run.ts --only 18`.
- All three R-12 acceptance criteria (affinity / drain / fan-out) green.
- Stretch sticky-killed-worker assertion passes or is filed as a follow-up.
- Existing scenarios 01-17 stay green (no regression in single-burrow boot).

title: R-13 — Bring-your-own database (Postgres backend)
description:
Implement ROADMAP R-13: support Postgres as an alternative storage backend so SREs at 50+ engineer orgs can attach warren to their managed Postgres instead of a docker volume.

Today src/db is SQLite-only — schema imports from drizzle-orm/sqlite-core, the client opens via drizzle-orm/bun-sqlite (a SYNCHRONOUS driver), 8 migrations under src/db/migrations/ use SQLite SQL (PRAGMA toggles, the 12-step ALTER pattern), and ~51 sync call sites (.run() / .get() / .all()) live across the repo layer + tests. Postgres via drizzle-orm/node-postgres is async-first — this is a driver-shape change as much as a schema-dialect change.

SPEC §6 currently lists bun:sqlite as the only backend; §3.2 lists 'no bring-your-own database' as a V1 non-goal. Scope here is to lift that non-goal, keep SQLite as the zero-config default, and add Postgres behind a WARREN_DB_URL env var without rewriting the burrow boundary (burrow keeps its own per-worker SQLite — that's run-local sandbox state, not org truth).

See ROADMAP.md R-13 for the spec sketch; plan owned by sd plan submit on this seed.

title: R-19 — Per-run preview environments
description: Each successful run optionally exposes a public URL of the form https://run-<id>.<warren-host> that proxies into the burrow workspace's listening port, so reviewers see a working app instead of just a diff. Tracks ROADMAP R-19. Marked [proposed]: needs design pass + burrow inbound-networking policy before it lands.

<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

Schema shape (must accept `type` discriminator from day one — see §11.L "Project opt-in shape"):

    preview:
      type: server          # 'server' ships in V1; 'static' rejected with "not yet implemented" naming the follow-up seed
      command: "bun run dev"
      port: 3000
      readiness_path: "/healthz"     # optional; defaults to GET /
      idle_ttl: "30m"                # optional; default from WARREN_PREVIEW_IDLE_TTL
      max_lifetime: "8h"             # optional; default from WARREN_PREVIEW_MAX_LIFETIME

Notes:
- Idle TTL and max-lifetime are **both** present in the schema. Do NOT collapse to a single `ttl` field — the design lock rejected wall-clock-only TTL because it yanks reviewers mid-session.
- `type: static` accepted by the parser but rejected at launch time with an error naming the follow-up seed (filed under pl-2c59).
- Missing `preview` block is not an error — projects opt in by adding it.
- Malformed `preview` surfaces in the per-file errors envelope (mx-66d478), same pattern as other `.warren/` fields.
- File location stays `.warren/defaults.json` for V1; the broader `.warren/` YAML reorg is a separate seed filed under pl-2c59.

title: Port allocator (SQLite-backed, restart-safe)
description:

Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Phase along the natural seam: lock the cross-repo design and burrow contract first, then ship the foundational layers (`.warren/defaults.json` schema, `runs` columns, port allocator) in parallel, then the reap-time launch sub-step that…

Run `sd plan show pl-2c59` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

SQLite-backed allocator, dialect-aware via the post-R-13 async repo layer (pl-f17e step 1).

**Restart-safety contract:**
On warren startup, in-use ports are derived from a SELECT against `runs` — NOT from in-memory state, NOT from a separate ports table:

    SELECT preview_port FROM runs WHERE preview_state IN ('starting','live') AND preview_port IS NOT NULL

The allocator query is **async** (returns a Promise) per the post-R-13 repo API. Allocator state survives `bun test --rerun` and process restart automatically because the source-of-truth is the runs table the eviction worker (step 7) already manipulates.

**Range + knobs:**
- Default range `30000-31000`, configurable via `WARREN_PREVIEW_PORT_RANGE`.
- Exhaustion emits a `preview_failed` event with `reason='port_exhausted'`.
- Doctor warning fires when ≥80% of the range is in use.

**Release path:**
The allocator does NOT track its own "released" set — release means setting `runs.preview_state` to `torn-down` or `failed` and clearing `runs.preview_port`. Next `SELECT preview_port WHERE preview_state IN ('starting','live')` naturally omits the released port. Single source of truth.

**Dialect concerns:**
- Query runs on both sqlite + postgres unchanged (no dialect-specific SQL).
- Concurrent allocation handled by the same per-backend transaction the eviction worker uses — SQLite uses `BEGIN IMMEDIATE`, postgres uses `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` on the candidate row. Test under both with `withDb()` (pl-f17e step 6).

title: Reap-time preview launch sub-step (5th best-effort step)
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 6 of plan pl-2c59.

Parent seed: warren-1bcb — R-19 — Per-run preview environments
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Phase along the natural seam: lock the cross-repo design and burrow contract first, then ship the foundational layers (`.warren/defaults.json` schema, `runs` columns, port allocator) in parallel, then the reap-time launch sub-step that…

Run `sd plan show pl-2c59` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

Adds **two** best-effort reap sub-steps, not one. Both mirror `pr_open`'s pattern (mx-05abb2): only fire on `outcome === 'succeeded'`, never fail the run, emit `reap_failed` events on error.

**Sub-step ordering at reap:**

1. `pr_open` (existing) — opens the PR. The PR body template emits a `<!-- warren:preview-placeholder -->` line via the `preview_url_or_placeholder` fragment when the project has opted into preview. Fragment is a no-op when not opted in.
2. `preview_launch` (new, this step) — asks burrow to spawn `preview.command` as a long-lived sidecar in the same workspace; allocates a port via the SQLite-backed allocator (step 5); polls `readiness_path` (or `GET /`) until 2xx or timeout. Transitions `preview_state: starting → live` on success; `failed` with stderr tail in `preview_failure_message` on failure.
3. `pr_annotate_preview` (new, this step) — once preview reaches `live` or `failed`, issues `PATCH /repos/.../pulls/:n` to replace the placeholder line with the URL (live) or failure tail (failed). Idempotent.

**Critical invariants:**

- PR open does **not** block on preview ready. Reviewers see the PR immediately; URL fills in async.
- Same burrow workspace as the agent run — not a fork. Side-effect risk is documented in §11.L.
- Failure of any sub-step never fails the run (`outcome` stays `succeeded`); emits `reap_failed` with `step` ∈ {`pr_open`, `preview_launch`, `pr_annotate_preview`}.
- The placeholder is the integration point for the broader PR-template configurability seed filed under pl-2c59 — keep the fragment name (`preview_url_or_placeholder`) stable.
- `preview_launch` requires `runs.worker_id === local` for V1; non-local workers skip with a `preview_failed` event referencing R-12.

title: TTL + LRU eviction worker
description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 7 of plan pl-2c59.

Parent seed: warren-1bcb — R-19 — Per-run preview environments
Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Phase along the natural seam: lock the cross-repo design and burrow contract first, then ship the foundational layers (`.warren/defaults.json` schema, `runs` columns, port allocator) in parallel, then the reap-time launch sub-step that…

Run `sd plan show pl-2c59` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

The eviction worker combines **four** signals, not two. Idle-TTL is the PRIMARY kill-rule — wall-clock-only TTL was explicitly rejected because it yanks reviewers mid-session.

**Signal 1 — Idle TTL (primary).**
Sweep evicts when `now - preview_last_hit_at > idle_ttl`. Default 30 min, overridable per-project (preview.idle_ttl) and globally (WARREN_PREVIEW_IDLE_TTL). `preview_last_hit_at` is maintained by the proxy (step 8) — see step 8 for the update-before-response + 30s-debounce contract.

**Signal 2 — Max lifetime ceiling.**
Hard cap from `preview_started_at` regardless of activity. Default 8h, overridable per-project (preview.max_lifetime) and globally (WARREN_PREVIEW_MAX_LIFETIME). Stops the "browser tab from yesterday is auto-refreshing the proxy" failure mode.

**Signal 3 — Global LRU cap.**
When `WARREN_PREVIEW_MAX_LIVE` (default 20) is reached and a new launch needs space, evict the longest-idle preview (smallest `preview_last_hit_at`). Bounds container memory regardless of TTL choices. `/readyz` emits a warning when live count > 80% of cap.

**Signal 4 — Manual teardown.**
Out of scope for this step (lives in step 9, `warren-d725`). The eviction worker MUST be re-entrant with manual teardown: a manual teardown that races with a scheduled idle-TTL eviction is idempotent on the row state.

**Common eviction action:**
Free the port (allocator-side release), stop the sidecar process via burrow, transition `preview_state → torn-down`, emit `preview_evicted` with `reason` ∈ {`idle_ttl`, `max_lifetime`, `lru`}. The **burrow workspace stays** — only the preview process dies.

description:
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:start -->
Step 8 of plan pl-2c59.

Plan template: feature
Plan approach: Phase along the natural seam: lock the cross-repo design and burrow contract first, then ship the foundational layers (`.warren/defaults.json` schema, `runs` columns, port allocator) in parallel, then the reap-time launch sub-step that…

Run `sd plan show pl-2c59` for the full plan (context, alternatives, sibling steps, acceptance criteria).
<!-- seeds:plan-backref:end -->

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

**In-process Bun route, not a separate reverse proxy.**
The Host-match preamble lives in `src/server/main.ts` *before* the API/UI routes. Matches `Host: run-<id>.<warren-host>`, resolves `runs.preview_port → 127.0.0.1:<port>`, forwards HTTP + WS through `burrowClientPool.clientFor({burrowId})` so multi-worker placement (warren-c0c9 / pl-9ba1 step 5) keeps holding.

**Update `last_hit_at` BEFORE returning the response — critical.**
A slow upstream response must not make the preview look idle. The proxy MUST write `runs.preview_last_hit_at = now()` before forwarding the request, not after the response completes. Debounce to ~once per 30s per run to keep the hot path cheap (in-memory last-write timestamp; flush to SQLite if >30s elapsed since last flush). This is the single load-bearing invariant the eviction worker (step 7) depends on.

**Signed-cookie auth, not bearer.**
Bearer-in-header is impossible for a browser hitting `run-<id>.<host>` directly. `GET /runs/:id/preview/login?token=<bearer>&redirect=<path>` validates the bearer, issues an HMAC-signed cookie scoped `Domain=.<warren-host>; Path=/; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax`, and redirects. Proxy preamble verifies the HMAC before forwarding. Unauthenticated requests **401** (not 200, not 502 — write the test).

**Cross-host (`runs.worker_id !== local`) returns 501.**
Explicit R-12 deferral message in the body so the failure is attributable, not "preview works for some runs, not others." Acceptance scenario 19 asserts this path.

**Doctor check.**
Warn when `WARREN_PREVIEW_HOST` is set but `WARREN_API_TOKEN` is the default placeholder — a misconfigured signed-cookie scope on a token-as-placeholder install turns a private-code preview into a public one.

## Design-lock contract (SPEC §11.L)

**Scenario number: 20, not 19.** Scenario 19 is `19-warren-on-postgres.ts` from pl-f17e step 7 (warren-480a, currently in flight). Preview scenario lands at `scripts/acceptance/scenarios/20-preview.ts`.

## Two variants in one file

**Variant A — Happy path:**
1. Dispatch a run against a project with `preview` opted in.
2. Agent succeeds; reap runs `pr_open` → `preview_launch` → `pr_annotate_preview` in order.
3. Assert: PR opened with placeholder line; preview reaches `live`; PR body patched with URL; idle TTL armed.
4. Assert: `Host: run-<id>.<host>` returns 200 through the proxy after signed-cookie auth; 401 without cookie.

**Variant B — Idle-TTL eviction:**
1. Same setup; force `WARREN_PREVIEW_IDLE_TTL=2s` for the scenario.
2. Wait past the idle window without making any proxy requests.
3. Assert: eviction worker transitions `preview_state → torn-down`; emits `preview_evicted` with `reason='idle_ttl'`; port returned to allocator.

## Dialect coverage

Per pl-f17e step 6 (warren-c823), the acceptance harness has dialect-aware substrate. Scenario 20 should run against **both** backends:

    WARREN_TEST_DIALECT=sqlite  ./scripts/acceptance/run.sh 20-preview
    WARREN_TEST_DIALECT=postgres ./scripts/acceptance/run.sh 20-preview

Both must pass. Preview state transitions, port allocator persistence, and the eviction query all touch the DB seam R-13 dual-tracks.

## Linux only (macOS skip)

Per mx-1d31f0, burrow's bwrap-based inbound networking is linux-only. macOS skips with a documented reason in the scenario header — same pattern as scenarios 13/14.

title: CI: auto-deploy to fly.io on release tag
description:
## Goal

Wire `.github/workflows/release.yml` to deploy warren to fly.io after the existing `release` job cuts a `v$VERSION` tag, so production tracks tagged releases (not every push to main).

## Why

- Warren is used to build warren; main moves fast, but prod should only run versioned/changelog-documented code.
- Today there is no CD — deploys are manual (`flyctl deploy` from a laptop).
- Target infra: fly.io container + Supabase Postgres. This issue is blocked on R-13 (warren-942c) shipping the Postgres backend, since the fly container needs to point `WARREN_DB_URL` at Supabase.

## Plan

Two edits to `.github/workflows/release.yml`:

1. Expose version-check decision as a job output on the existing `release` job:

```yaml
jobs:
  release:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
    outputs:
      released: ${{ steps.version-check.outputs.release }}
      version:  ${{ steps.version-check.outputs.version }}
    steps:
      # existing steps unchanged
```

2. Append a new `deploy` job:

```yaml
  deploy:
    needs: release
    if: needs.release.outputs.released == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    concurrency:
      group: fly-deploy-production
      cancel-in-progress: false
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v6
        with:
          ref: v${{ needs.release.outputs.version }}
      - uses: superfly/flyctl-actions/setup-flyctl@master
      - run: flyctl deploy --remote-only --strategy rolling
        env:
          FLY_API_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.FLY_API_TOKEN }}
```

## Design notes

- `needs: release` + `if: released == 'true'` — only deploys when a tag was actually cut. Skips workflow_dispatch re-runs and pushes that do not bump `package.json` + `src/index.ts`.
- `ref: v${{ version }}` — checks out the tag, not main HEAD. Guarantees the deployed image matches the GitHub release even if main moves between tag push and deploy step.
- `concurrency: cancel-in-progress: false` — back-to-back releases queue rather than cancelling an in-flight rolling restart.
- `--remote-only` — build on Fly builder, not the GH runner. Matches Fly docs.
- `--strategy rolling` — explicit (default depends on `fly.toml`). Single-machine safe; revisit `bluegreen` if scaled out.

## Pre-merge setup

- Create a fly deploy token scoped to the warren app: `fly tokens create deploy -a <app>`. Do NOT reuse the user-scoped token from `flyctl auth token`.
- Add as repo secret: `FLY_API_TOKEN` (Settings → Secrets → Actions).
- Confirm `fly.toml` is shaped for prod (image name, regions, mounts for any state, [http_service] section). Today's `fly.toml` exists at repo root but predates the Supabase plan — review before first deploy.

## Acceptance

1. Pushing a non-version-bump commit to main runs `release` job, which short-circuits (`release=false`); `deploy` job is skipped (visible as "skipped" in the run UI, not "failed").
2. Bumping `package.json` + `src/index.ts` to a new version on main:
   - `release` job tags `vX.Y.Z`, creates GitHub release from CHANGELOG.
   - `deploy` job checks out `vX.Y.Z`, runs `flyctl deploy --remote-only`, exits 0.
   - `fly status` shows the new release ID; `curl https://<app>.fly.dev/healthz` reports the new VERSION.
3. Two version bumps merged back-to-back: deploys run serially (queued by concurrency group), neither cancels the other.
4. A broken release (e.g., failing migration on first boot against Supabase) leaves the previous machine running because `--strategy rolling` will not promote a failing replacement. Document the rollback recipe (`fly releases list` + `fly deploy --image <prev-image>`) in CLAUDE.md or DEPLOY.md.

## Out of scope

- Preview environments per-PR or per-run (that is R-19, pl-2c59 — warren-1bcb).
- Staging environment. Single prod app for now; revisit if needed.
- Supabase migration automation (Postgres migrations apply on warren boot per R-13 step 5; no separate Supabase CLI step needed).

## Files

- Edit: `.github/workflows/release.yml` (the two changes above)
- Review/verify: `fly.toml`
- Maybe edit: `CLAUDE.md` or new `DEPLOY.md` (rollback recipe, secret setup checklist)
